title: 2025/06/17 - MA
displayTitle: 2025-06-17 - Māori Affairs Committee - 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Tari Whakatau
description: 6 June 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Tari Whakatau Minister for Treaty of Waitangi Negotiations Hon Paul Goldsmith Te Tari Whakatau Justine Smith, Tumu Whakarae Warren Fraser, Deputy Chief Executive Tui Marsh, Deputy Chief Executive Matthew Andrews, Chief Legal Advisor Fern Hyett, Director Leah Campbell, Director Mikaere Paki, Chief Advisor Marama Ellis, Principal Advisor Joleen MacLean, Finance Business Partner
descriptionSummary: 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Tari Whakatau. Minister for Treaty of Waitangi Negotiations Hon Paul Goldsmith. Te Tari Whakatau

Well, uh, look, I, I, ultimately, there, yes, as you know, I mean, uh, the, the government's view has always been, uh, very clear on this, that the, um, uh, that the, the sovereignty was established by a process of, of constitutional and jurisdictional steps, uh, sign, uh, starting with the signing of the treaty, uh, followed by, um, the proclamations by Governor Hobson in May, 1840, and the gazing of the proclamations in May, 1840.

Uh, I've been, uh, working my way through that processes, um, as diligently as we can from outside of the equation. And, um, we are hopeful, uh, that we'll be able to get some resolution and, um, uh, get those, uh, squared away, uh, as soon as we can. Uh, um, Look, look, look, uh, What's our, I mean, our starting position, uh, on all of this is, um, uh, you know, a determination to, um, acknowledge, Uh, Treaty settlements, uh, and, uh, commitments made in the treaty and, and, you know, including discussions around customary rights, uh, while never losing sight of the basic expectations of New Zealanders to live in a, uh, uh, um, in a modern democratic setting, uh, where all New Zealanders will sort of have quality before the law,

and broadly speaking, a, an equal say in what happens in their environment. So, uh, uh, we're very conscious that all New Zealanders have an interest in what happens in the coastal area. Uh, and, uh, the, the Trai Moana regime in so far is that, um, could lead to the granting of customary marine title, um, you know, materially affects that.

Uh, it gives the ability of, um, somebody, uh, a group that has customary marine title to, um, uh, make decisions in, in relation to resource management seats, uh, on a bunch of areas. So, um, uh, our view is that Parliament, when it passed, the legislation always had in mind a very high threshold, uh, for that, um, which had two legs, one, based on TIG Young, and the second one based on, um, uh, um, continuous, um, uh, uh, possession since 1840 and occupational possession.

So, um, the Court of Appeal, uh, materially reduced that threshold. Uh, we've introduced legislation to overturn that. Uh, then before that legislation was concluded, the Supreme Court came in, and, uh, the government is still considering, uh, its, uh, options in response to that. And I, I can't give you a, a, um, uh, a government decision on, on, on that, but we're, we, are, we are actively considering that at the moment.

Uh, do you think it's fair, the activation that your introduction of the legislation before the Supreme Court finding was a knee jerk reaction, and in fact, the many, many, um, wise commentators and observers suggested that it would've been better to Tahoe and wait and see what the Supreme Court says, and in fact, that the Supreme Court found in favor of the Attorney General's appeal?

Uh, no. I wouldn't say it was knee jerk. Um, it, it was, uh, it was, you know, it was interesting that the Supreme Court, um, uh, um, came to a conclusion so swiftly, um, uh, on, on that element. But, uh, um, uh, no, we, we, we felt that we need to be clear about that.

And so we, we have had three courts with three different interpretations of the legislation. Uh, and so, um, obviously we're working our way through our response, which, you know, could range from, uh, leaving it as it is following the Supreme Court, or, uh, carrying on with the, uh, the legislation that's been introduced in some amended form, recognizing the Supreme Court decisions.

And that's something that the government's working through at the moment. What are the odds that you'll leave it as it is? I won't put any odds on it, Jenny. Thank you. Um, what contingencies, if any, uh, have you allocated for litigation against Contingencies? Uh, well, it's built into a budget. Well, uh, we, we built it into the, into the budget.

Any responses come to the mic who's got people online. Thank you. Um, interested to know why you chose to ignore the Y Tangi Tribunal suggestion that the government should halt all efforts to amend KU and also, sorry, Should halt all what, sorry. Well, there was a Ang Tribunal, um, uh, recommendation that the government should halt all efforts to amend Kuana.

Uh, that, that's a very different, um, thing to ignoring it. So, we are a, we're a, we are a nation in a culture, uh, um, that, um, obviously loves, uh, the coastline and is very interacting with it, uh, and live alongside it. They have, um, and, and so everybody's got an interest. And so what we're trying, the legislation was trying to sort of pitch that at a particular, uh, level with a, with a high threshold before customer marine title is granted.

Uh, and, um, so it's, you know, very much within the preserve of government to, um, uh, have legislation in that space. Well, Uh, look, I'm not gonna put a timeframe on it, but we are, we're, we're actively turning our mind to it.

And we're, we're conscious of the fact that, um, you know, we do want to, uh, resolve these issues, uh, sooner rather than later. Uh, and, you know, we're broad, broadly speaking, we are concerned about the whole, um, uh, framework, uh, that we've, um, that that's developed. Uh, uh, you know, I am worried that, um, you know, we could see that the way that it's currently set up, we could be having, um, court cases for many, many years ahead on, on, um, these issues.

Uh, and we could still be debating, uh, debating it in 2040. And, uh, so I'm worried about that. And so we are, you know, actively turning our mind to the, to how, how we can come up with a more, uh, efficient process, uh, so that people can get on things. What work has, uh, undertaken to review the settings and the operating model, uh, to manage costs within the app? Well, uh, well, um, my, my colleagues might like to comment on, on that a bit more, but, uh, the, the reality was that we, uh, it was unsustainable, um, as it, uh, evolved when I, I came the pre you, the previous government, um, allocated I think $12 million a year for, um, uh, contribution to costs.

Uh, when I, I took over the role, um, the projections was that, that it was gonna blow out to 30 million. Uh, and, um, uh, our government did not believe that that was sustainable, you know, given the fact that we could be, you know, facing 20 years of this. Uh, and so we've instructed, um, uh, um, uh, a need to have a, um, to, to stick to the broader budget.

Uh, and that is challenging. And so we had, um, uh, so I think we've lifted it to 13 million, broadly speaking, uh, for the next financial year. But it is challenging, and that's why we certainly are turning our mind to the whole framework, uh, to see if there's, uh, not a better way to do it. How, Who have you consulted as a part of that work? Well, it's not part of ongoing con con conversations, but we haven't made any, uh, particular decisions around, uh, that, but before we do, we'll certainly be consulting right across the board.

Mm-hmm. But I don't know if there's anything you wanted to add on that? No. Have you consulted with any claimant groups as, as part of that process? Um, uh, in terms of formal changes to the process? 'cause we haven't made any decisions around that we haven't made, um, and have formal consultations around that.
